issue_title: Have a duckpy context that lasts for more than more tha one .evaljs() call
issue_body:
Hi @amol-,  

this is a feature request, not a bug.

As you maybe remember, i'm using duckpy with babeljs to compile ES6+ javascript to ES5 js. 
In this use case, evaluating the babeljs minified source code which is 750 KB  takes time and this slows down the overall per-file translation time. If the number of code blocks (or files) to translate is known this can be resolved with an evaljs() wrapper that handles such multiple blocks.  Unfortunately this doesn't cover all the use cases where it would be nice to have a js interpreter already "hot", waiting for translation job... but let me contextualize a bit.

I'm using duckpy together with [metapensiero.pj (AKA Javascripthon)
](https://github.com/azazel75/metapensiero.pj) to transpile anything  ranging from a tree of Python 3.5 source files to a code object  (a class, a function) to ES6+ JS and then to ES5 with babeljs. 

Having an "hot" duckpy interpreter, with all the babeljs code already loaded would mean that converting and serving  python-ish code to the client becomes possible if the client has loaded the dependencies already.

issue_title: next_below gets confused by subnormal values
issue_body:
Given the following code:
>import gmpy2
>from gmpy2 import mpfr
>
>def fmt(v):
>    return '{:.23b}'.format(v)
>
>ctx32 = gmpy2.ieee(32)
>ctx32.subnormalize = True
>
>gmpy2.set_context(ctx32)
>
>a = mpfr('0x1p-126')
>b = gmpy2.next_below(a)
>
>print 'a=', a, 'b=', b
>print 'a=', fmt(a), 'b=', fmt(b)

the program prints the same values for `a` and `b`.  The value `a` is set here to the smallest normal value, but the same happens for subnormal values.

I suspect there are several bugs here: if I use `ctx32.subnormalize = False` the proper (subnormal) number is returned, so the meaning of the flag seems to be reversed, but if subnormals are not supported, `next_below` should return 0 rather than the same value.

Also, it would be nice if gmpy2 would support the function `nextafter(x, y)`.

Finally, it would be great if the `gmpy2.ieee()` function would support an extra parameter to create an IEEE-compliant context without subnormal support.

```python
from math import sqrt
from pint import _DEFAULT_REGISTRY
ureg = _DEFAULT_REGISTRY
Q_ = ureg.Quantity

@ureg.wraps(ureg.second, (ureg.meters, ureg.meters/ureg.second**2))
def calculate_time_to_fall(height, gravity=9.8):
    """Calculate time to fall from a height h.

    By default, the gravity is assumed to be earth gravity,
    but it can be modified.

    d = .5 * g * t**2
    t = sqrt(2 * d / g)

    return sqrt(2 * height / gravity)

```

issue_title: more English please
Support for running behind a reverse proxy
issue_body:
I tried to run spotmop behind a reverse proxy. This seems quite hard to accomplish. What needs to be done:

- URLs need to be relative to the current url. Often they are absolute to the server. (Workaround: Use it on a subdomain instead of subfolder)
- The Port of the server should be used (works mostly, seems to have problems with websockets)
- HTTPs URLs need to be used if spotmop is requested using https.

But the biggest problem I saw is requesting mopidy at 6681 without a way to configure urls or use rewriting. Either it should request localhost:6681 on serverside or allow to configure a url which can be a reverse proxy to server:6681. Just requesting with the port isn't feasible for such a web-setup, which should NOT expose the server directly.

issue_title: MemoryError on Exception
issue_body: When using parmap.map(func, par_list, par1, par2, par3, par4) in a loop, a MemoryError can occur if there is an Exception thrown inside func, which is handled outside of func. The new threads are not closed correctly this way and thus parmap spawns more and more threads (loop) until the RAM is full. This was not an easy one to find ;-)

Investigate Verification of OCSP Response/Signature
issue_body:
See page 27 of docs:
https://www.bankid.com/assets/bankid/rp/riktlinjer-foer-mobilt-bankid-till-foerlitande-part-1-0-7.pdf

The docs don't explain it that well, but the collect response contains a signature from the client, and a OCSP response by what I'm guessing is the bank which signed the client's certificate. 

> The OCSP 0 response is signed by a certificate that has the same issuer as the certificate being 
> verified (i.e. the BankId).

I think the purpose for this is that it gives the bank power to revoke the user certificate. So the infrastructure in that sense is reliant on both the bank and the BankID backend. I think the bank controls the OCSP stapling, while BankID stands for the authentication infrastructure. 

I found some old documentation of the server architecture for the Norwegian system from 2005:
http://www.eurim.org.uk/activities/pi/BankIDWhitePaper.pdf

So there seems to be the root-CA which is jointly owned by all the banks. Then each bank gets its own level-1 CA signed by the root. 

Question is, do we need to verify these as a merchant? Why would they include them in the response otherwise?

issue_title: Running at a specific time weekly calls job twice.
issue_body:
We ran into a minor bug when scheduling `schedule.every().sunday.at("6:00").do(myfunc)` -- `myfunc` is called twice because next run is not updated correctly. 

This is clearly difficult to give a test case for unless it happens to be near the time the schedule is set to run for, so we used [freezegun]() to mock `datetime.datetime.now` to increment time in a meaningful way:

```python
import schedule

from freezegun import freeze_time
from unittest.mock import MagicMock
from datetime import datetime, timedelta

def test_schedule_weekly():
    mockTask = MagicMock()
    m = lambda: mockTask(datetime.now())

    start = datetime(2017, 1, 1, 0, 0)
    until = datetime(2018, 1, 1, 0, 0)
    with freeze_time(start) as fzdt:

        scheduler = schedule.Scheduler()
        scheduler.every().sunday.at("6:00").do(m)

        while datetime.now() < until:
            fzdt.tick(delta=timedelta(minutes=15))
            scheduler.run_pending()
            import pdb; pdb.set_trace()

    assert mockTask.call_count == 52 # assert failure 106 != 52
```

This issue could, of course, be due to freezegun, but we should say that updating our code to `scheduler.every().sunday.do(m)` passed the tests.  We think it's something to do with `at` and `_schedule_next_run`, which is a bit complicated (RE #116). 

We traced out the scheduler.jobs after each call to `run_pending` and found that next run was not updated correctly when the job was run at its given time, but then was updated when the job ran after its scheduled time, here's the trace: 

At Sunday, January 1, 2017, 5:45am:

```
>>> scheduler.jobs 
[Every 1 week at 06:00:00 do <lambda>() (last run: [never], next run: 2017-01-01 06:00:00)]
```

At Sunday, January 1, 2017, 6:00am:

```
>>> scheduler.jobs
[Every 1 week at 06:00:00 do <lambda>() (last run: 2017-01-01 06:00:00, next run: 2017-01-01 06:00:00)]
```

And at Sunday, January 1, 2017, 6:15am:

```
>>> scheduler.jobs 
[Every 1 week at 06:00:00 do <lambda>() (last run: 2017-01-01 06:15:00, next run: 2017-01-08 06:00:00)]
```

We're happy to submit a PR - but it would be useful if someone could point us in the right direction!

issue_title: RarInfo not agreeing with parameters needed to pass to the extraction step; use libunrar instead of running command-line unrar, and non-unicode filenames
issue_body:
RarInfo not agreeing with parameters needed to pass to the extraction step seem to defeat the purpose of looking at RarInfo  first.

Use libunrar instead of running command-line unrar for non-unicode filenames might be the answer.

I have a rar file which seems to have been generated on localised CJK windows system (i.e. filenames are multi-byte but non-unicode).

RarInfo.filename gives the following hexdumps with ` ' '.join(["%04X"% ord(x) for x in info.filename])`

```
E5A4 A9B9 72A6 ACAB
E5A4 A9B9 72A6 ACAB 535C 4E49 3030 504D 542E 4654
00A4 00E5 00B9 00A9 00A6 0072 00AB 00AC 005C 0057 0045 0049 0030 0030 0042 002E 0054 0054 0046
```

rarfile seems to interprete the data as UTF16-LE  or latin1, but in fact it is BIG5 (and big-endian - higher-byte of big5 encoding first). You can see the 3rd item is padded and byte-swapped compared to the others.

I also don't know why rarfile is behaving inconsistently - one file is in latin1, but all the others are in UTF16-LE, in the same archive.

Anyway, I have two problems:

command-line unrar munches the paths - in the name of 'security measure', it would not extract filename as they were, or even list them as rawbytes, but insists that they are unicode and insert a lot of '0xE0' with private encoding in the path names when the byte ranges is outside unicode descriptions. (see `unrar/unicode.cpp` in unrar's source).

And you are running unrar the command line, which means there is no hope of matching the `RarInfo.filename` with what is required on the command line (to extract then rename), since `RarInfo.filename` contains raw non-unicode multibyte date, while command line wants corrupted unicode data.

FWIW, `lsar -e BIG5 ...` seems to do the right thing. But I just like `python-rarfile` to be able to do that, and give me more control of examining and extracting the files individually.

RarInfo not agreeing with parameters needed to pass to the extraction step seem to defeat the purpose of looking at RarInfo  first.

issue_title: Post install task not properly executed when being installed from PyPi
issue_body:
Affected version: `v0.9.8.4` and below

Verbose installation from PyPi:
```bash
sudo -H pip install evo --upgrade -vvv
```
shows no output of the [post install task](https://github.com/MichaelGrupp/evo/blob/5c4314a1858587916bdf1eddc1f4ec8c8350554d/setup.py#L32).

---

Verbose installation from source:
```bash
sudo -H pip install . --upgrade -vvv
```
shows the correct output of the post install task.

---

This implies that argcomplete and iPython are not properly initialized from a PyPi installation - investigate.

issue_title: Jedi cache directory on Windows (AppData\Roaming)
issue_body: The default jedi cache directory is the roaming folder. In my company, we have restrictions on the file size of the Roaming folder (since I believe these settings are synced across computers). The jedi cache really isn't a fundamental user setting that needs to be synced across devices, so it really should be in the local app data folder. So I propose here that the default windows path use the environment variable `%LocalAppData%` instead.

issue_title: has_role() function only recognizes roles by name.
issue_body: Currently, has_role() within commands > core.py only recognizes roles by their name as a string. This allows a user to create their own discord server and add a duplicate role which then allows the bot to have the same privileges on the original server thereby gaining a privilege escalation. In order to prevent this, has_role() should also accept role ID as an argument as the role IDs would be different between servers.

issue_title: Default alerts for io wait on CPU plugin seem wrong
issue_body:
#### Description

IO Wait on Linux is a form of idle time. Its an indication that the processor is idle while one or more process is doing I/O. It doesn't necessarily indicate a problem, unless that I/O is itself taking a long time. This is a good description of it:

https://blog.pregos.info/wp-content/uploads/2010/09/iowait.txt

Given this information, I'm not sure that the following default configuration of the CPU plugin makes sense:

```
iowait_careful=50
iowait_warning=70
iowait_critical=90
```

On a very fast mostly idle system, writing to rotational disks often causes Glances to produce critical CPU_IOWAIT alerts, simply because writing to rotational disks is slow and the CPU is doing nothing else at the time. These alerts don't actually mean anything is wrong though.

#### Versions

```
# glances -V
Glances v2.7.1 with psutil v5.0.1
```

issue_title: Refresh Tokens
issue_body:
I appreciate the work on this project.

Currently any expired access token can be used along with the refresh token, to gain a new access token.

Instead would it make sense to represent the refresh_token as a JWT itself, such that:
- The user info can be added into the token, instead of having to use an unverified access token.
- We can add an expiry time to the refresh token (currently lasts forever, unless the application code adds TTL logic in it's store).

Thanks

  File "/usr/local/var/pyenv/versions/anaconda3-4.2.0/lib/python3.5/site-packages/pydicom/dataset.py", line 709, in pixel_array
    return self._get_pixel_array()
  File "/usr/local/var/pyenv/versions/anaconda3-4.2.0/lib/python3.5/site-packages/pydicom/dataset.py", line 695, in _get_pixel_array
    self._pixel_array = self._compressed_pixel_data_numpy()
  File "/usr/local/var/pyenv/versions/anaconda3-4.2.0/lib/python3.5/site-packages/pydicom/dataset.py", line 583, in _compressed_pixel_data_numpy
    UncompressedPixelData = self._get_PIL_supported_compressed_pixeldata()
  File "/usr/local/var/pyenv/versions/anaconda3-4.2.0/lib/python3.5/site-packages/pydicom/dataset.py", line 648, in _get_PIL_supported_compressed_pixeldata
    UncompressedPixelData += decompressed_image.tobytes()
TypeError: Can't convert 'bytes' object to str implicitly
```

I believe this can be resolved when using `bytearray` or `b''` in dataset.py, like so:

```python
def _get_PIL_supported_compressed_pixeldata(self):
        if not have_pillow:
            msg = "The pillow package is required to use pixel_array for this transfer syntax {0}, and pillow could not be imported.".format(self.file_meta.TransferSyntaxUID)
            raise ImportError(msg)
        # decompress here
        if self.file_meta.TransferSyntaxUID in pydicom.uid.JPEGLossyCompressedPixelTransferSyntaxes:
            if self.BitsAllocated > 8:
                raise NotImplementedError("JPEG Lossy only supported if Bits Allocated = 8")
            generic_jpeg_file_header = b'\xff\xd8\xff\xe0\x00\x10JFIF\x00\x01\x01\x01\x00\x01\x00\x01\x00\x00'
            frame_start_from = 2
        elif self.file_meta.TransferSyntaxUID in pydicom.uid.JPEG2000CompressedPixelTransferSyntaxes:
            generic_jpeg_file_header = b''
            # generic_jpeg_file_header = b'\x00\x00\x00\x0C\x6A\x50\x20\x20\x0D\x0A\x87\x0A'
            frame_start_from = 0
        else:
            generic_jpeg_file_header = b''
            frame_start_from = 0
        try:
            UncompressedPixelData = bytearray()
            if 'NumberOfFrames' in self and self.NumberOfFrames > 1:
                # multiple compressed frames
                CompressedPixelDataSeq = pydicom.encaps.decode_data_sequence(self.PixelData)
                for frame in CompressedPixelDataSeq:
                    data = generic_jpeg_file_header + frame[frame_start_from:]
                    fio = io.BytesIO(data)
                    try:
                        decompressed_image = PILImg.open(fio)
                    except IOError as e:
                        raise NotImplementedError(e.message)
                    UncompressedPixelData += decompressed_image.tobytes()
            else:
                # single compressed frame
                UncompressedPixelData = pydicom.encaps.defragment_data(self.PixelData)
                UncompressedPixelData = generic_jpeg_file_header + UncompressedPixelData[frame_start_from:]
                try:
                    fio = io.BytesIO(UncompressedPixelData)
                    decompressed_image = PILImg.open(fio)
                except IOError as e:
                    raise NotImplementedError(e.message)
                UncompressedPixelData = decompressed_image.tobytes()
        except:
            raise
        return bytes(UncompressedPixelData)

issue_title: Provide output in an OrderedDict to keep sorting
issue_body:
Carrying on from #12, I think it would make sense to always use an OrderedDict everywhere in Pyrebase. Firebase use the ordering of the elements in the JSON response as a response to the sorting requested in the request, but the standard Python dictionary does not keep the ordering, which means we currently implement our own sorting on top of what Firebase does.

If we instead decode the JSON ourselves, using the `object_pairs_hook` kwarg, we can supply that with an OrderedDict as shown [here](http://stackoverflow.com/a/6921760/878291), which will keep the ordering, and thus require less work on our end since we don't need to sort the elements again.

I think the only handling we need of the response from Firebase then would be the following:

1. Is the JSON decoded response something else than a dict/OrderedDict? If yes, then simply return it.
2. Are we doing a `shallow` request? If yes, then only return the keys of the dictionary.

What are your thoughts on this?

issue_title: class Meta - a cleaner future?
issue_body:
I was having a think the last few days about py3status modules.

currently a module has update methods like this

```
 def update(self, i3s_output_list, i3s_config):
```
but I've never seen any that use `output_list`, and `i3s_config` only seems to be used to get color states.
```
 def update(self):
```
would be much nicer, but has two problems:

1) All the modules would need updating or would break including users custom modules

2) we now can't do `color_degraded` etc

But we have the current opportunity to solve both these issues and move to a cleaner calling pattern.
We can do this by using the `class Meta:`  Currently employed by the group module. This is what I would like to do.

If a Py3status module contains `class Meta:` it is taken to signify that it works with the new 'standard'.  As such it would have update calls of just `update(self)` without the parameters.  The `on_click` would likewise be `on_click(self, event):`  So we have the simplified calling but only for self-declaring modules.  All existing modules would continue to function as before.

But what about the `i3s_config` that might be useful to a module?  Well, firstly the module can still access that config.

```
    class Meta:
        include_py3_module = True
```

gives access the modules `Module` and we could easily add a `helper_get_i3_config()` method.  So that would solve that issue, although I'm not sure that the config is that useful. Apart from the `color_bad` type stuff.

With the colors many modules do 
```
 response['color'] = i3s_config['color_bad']
```
some allow custom degraded colors to be defined in their config (which is nicer)
```
response['color'] = self.color_bad or i3s_config['color_bad']
```

But I want to go further, what if my module just did
```
 response['color'] = 'bad'
```
and we then automagically change this to a config specific `color_bad` if it is supplied, else the general `color_bad`.  If an actual color is returned then we honor eg
```
 response['color'] = '#FF00FF'
```

I'd also like to change the `cache_until` so that setting it to `-1` would mean cache forever (well until an update is forced eg middle click), but again just for new style modules.

So to convert a module you would update the method arguments etc and then just add

```
class Meta:
    pass
```

Once we have this in place we can make more changes easily in the future.  eg.

```
class Meta:
    use_groovy_new_events = True
```

So this is my suggestion.  I'm interested to know what people think of it.  I could easily knock up a patch to demonstrate this but I'll wait until either there is some interest in this idea or it has been shot down in flames.

issue_title: natsort with ns.LOCAL error: ValueError: character U+110000 is not in range [U+0000; U+10ffff]
issue_body:
This isn't really a bug in ``natsort``, but rather with the UTF-8 implementation on BSD systems (like OSX).  See http://stackoverflow.com/questions/33459384/unicode-character-not-in-range-when-calling-locale-strxfrm details into the cause of the issue.

I am creating this issue and leaving it open so that users that see this problem know that they need to use [PyICU](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/PyICU) to solve this problem if they see it.

issue_title: Sending "Ctrl-C" hotkey combination does not work
issue_body:
I've been searching for a solution to this for several days, but am beginning to wonder if I'm just looking at a bug.

I have a script that needs to copy to the clipboard using Ctrl + C, but somehow sending this key combination has lost functionality in pyautogui.

There is no exception thrown, and it appears everything is executing as it should be. However, the desired text is not copied to the clipboard. Copying the text manually using Ctrl + C (on my actual keyboard) does cause the desired text to be copied. However, when running pyautogui the clipboard remains unchanged.

I found this question on Stack Overflow that verifies the issue:
http://stackoverflow.com/questions/38899566/python-pyautogui-and-ctrl-c?newreg=4a6bdd511c314b129438d6d79f427600

I am also running Windows 7 and Python 3.5, as is Todd Lewden, the author of the Stack Overflow question.

The "Cheat Sheet" on Read the Docs indicates that this functionality should be present in pyautogui:

> Keyboard hotkeys like Ctrl-S or Ctrl-Shift-1 can be done by passing a list of key names to hotkey():
> 
> > > > pyautogui.hotkey('ctrl', 'c')  # ctrl-c to copy
> > > > pyautogui.hotkey('ctrl', 'v')  # ctrl-v to paste

I'm not sure at what point sending the "Ctrl + C" combination became broken, but it looks like it worked at some point, according to the above reference to the documentation. 

I had a thought that perhaps it has to do with "Ctrl + C" being a Python keyboard interrupt, but I don't know that that is the case. If it were, Python should come to a screeching halt when the Ctrl + C is sent. It appears to execute without error, except that it doesn't work.

>>> from plumbum.cmd import echo

A link like the following would cause an error/warning in Excel:

worksheet.write_url(0, 0, url, 'external:C:\Temp\Test 1')

issue_title: Compatibility with other tools
issue_body:
It'd be nice for this to support compatibility with `rvm` and `avn` out of the box to avoid having to [hack in compatibility manually](https://github.com/kennethreitz/autoenv/issues/45).

There's a [small library](https://github.com/mpapis/bash_zsh_support) that easily allows this type of compatibility and is easy to integrate.

issue_title: Allow knowing which jobs are running (and not just submitted/pending and paused)
issue_body:
It seems like we can pretty easily know by looking at the job.next_run_time whether a job is paused or not but it is really hard to know if a job is running or not (as far as a I can tell) since there is no apparent job state that is connected to a job saying the job is actively running (the event notification system is not reliable enough to do this, as it can be fired out of order).

So this is more of a feature request to have such a capability...

If more details are needed (or I can suggest some ideas on how to do it or maybe even a PR).

issue_title: Evaluating other features
issue_body:
This is just a place where I can jot notes on other regular expression features.

- One thing to note, if Re and Regex were to ever use `\C` or `\c`, we'd have to drop the lower case and upper case property shorthand of `\l`, `\c`, `\L`, and `\C` in searches.  Since we have things like `[[:upper:]]` or `\p{Lu}` etc., I don't see this as a problem.  There are two regular expression features that if added could break this though, but I don't particularly care about either one of those features.  But it should be noted that they *could* be added in Re or Regex regardless of how I "feel" about them.

    The question is, do we drop this shorthand of `l` and `c`?  We can't really use `\u` due to how Python uses `\u`  PCRE variants usually specify Unicode as `\u{...}` which would make this possible, but just isn't the case in Python.  I like the shorthand, but I am not going to fight for the shorthand.  I think this got borrowed from boost?

    Anyways, dropping the upper and lower character class shorthand is a real question to consider if we want to add `\cK` in the future, or if Re or Regex does.

- Locale awareness.  I don't currently take into consideration the locale flag for anything.  I don't plan on augmenting any character classes when the locale flag is set.

- Collating sequence seem to be a locale dependent feature, and as previously stated, I don't really have an interest in this area. This isn't something that is seen in every engine anyways.  If I ever got the itch to dig into locale stuff, maybe my opinion on this would change.

- `\cK` etc.  I personally don't care about this.  I'd rather just specify the bytes.  I don't see `regex` adding it as he's had plenty of time to do so, mentions that regex has enough features, and I just don't see the big usefulness of this.

- `\C` allows grabbing a "unit".  So in UTF16 mode it grab 16 bits etc.  I feel this is another feature I don't particularly care about.  I don't see us having modes in Python regex to change unit types, but you never know. In Re and Regex `.` essentially does what I think `\C` would do.  This seems like a dead end escape for Python.

- `\v` and `\V`.  Python uses `\v` for vertical tabs. That is built into strings, so I don't see a reason to override that logic. Since Python regex is in raw strings you **could** have it, but I'd have to see Re make that step first, and if it did, it would make adding this ourselves moot.

- `\h` and `H`.  If we aren't providing a references for vertical whitespace, it would be weird to add one for horizontal whitespace.  So this would be a pass as well.

- ~~`\p` in byte strings. We don't allow these only because it isn't practical.  There are some properties that contain only characters beyond the byte string limit. If you specify one of these groups, how do you represent a character group that captures characters that cannot be represented? You can't just truncate as it will be an invalid, empty character group.  Again, it just isn't practical.  I could allow only certain groups that can be represented in both the normal and inverse without issues, but that would just be confusing to people.  Instead we just allow `[[:posix:]]` style representation in byte strings, all of which can be represented properly even when truncating them to 0x00 - 0xFF.~~ Added.

- ~~`\e` for escape character.  This is kind of in the same category as `\cK` etc. in that I don't really care about it, but I have nothing that conflicts with it, so...maybe.~~ Added.

- ~~`\<` and `\>` for start and end word boundary respectively.  Essentially `\<` equals `\b(?=\w)` and `\>` equals `\b(?<=\w)`.  I've seen this in some engines, but I rarely have a need for such a pattern.  But I guess it could be useful, but I have no immediate plans to add such a feature.  I'd probably skip the other format I've seen `[[:<:]]` and `[[:>:]]`.~~ Added as `\m` and `\M` like Regex does.

- ~~`\X` for grapheme clusters.  It is not possible to implement true grapheme clusters using the grapheme cluster break property, but we can use a form roughly equivalent to the simplified form of `(?>\PM\pM*)` that regular expression engines (like Perl) used to use. It's better than nothing, and can still be kind of useful. This would be only for Re as Regex has a proper `\X` already.~~ Added a simplified form of `\X` for Re.

I haven't seen anything else that stands out as something that is common enough to add, or potentially useful enough.  There are some features that would be really cool in Re, but can only realistically be implemented in the engine core.

issue_title: consuming events in a recurring loop rather than on occurrence?
issue_body:
I find myself not wanting to act on every event as it occurs but instead queue them up for timed processing.   This is also because the first time we try to act on something, it might fail, and we need to retry it later.  Need to track events to retry as well.

Watchdog already has queues and allows for a parent task to be doing other activities, so I created
a 'event_sleep()' to run every sleep seconds.  Demo attached (just a modification of the sample provided in the documentation with event_sleep)  so now the event handlers are just adding events to my own queue.

[w2.txt](https://github.com/gorakhargosh/watchdog/files/690822/w2.txt)   (rename to .py to run)

This works fine (and because of details out of scope, have achieved many-fold speedup as a result.) No problems so far. I copy the new events to a pending list at the beginning of each round of timed processing.  I note that there is potential for events to be missed/dropped if they are added to the new_events list after the copy has occurred, but before it has been emptied.   It is tiny, but it could happen.

I was thinking that it would make more sense not to dispatch the events at all, but instead let them queue up between timed events, and have some sort of api call to take an event from the queue, without any need for any event handler.  Indeed I don't want the events handled, just queued for the periodic processing. That would eliminate the tiny race condition, but I don't see how to do it.

I would imagine something like:
    observer.schedule( None, path, recursive=True)

to say that I wanted nothing to handle events, then issue the start.
then something like like...

   e = observer.consume()
   the would be non-blocking, returning an event from the top of the queue if available
   None otherwise.

Is there an easy way to do something like that?

issue_title: XXE Injection
issue_body:
xml.parsers library is known to be vulnerable to XXE based attacks (CVE-2017-9233 for EX)
Therefore, the developer of this library decided to use the  defusedexpat.pyexpat wrapper which is the secure fork of original Expat library.

> defusedexpat protects the XML packages of Python's standard library from
several denial of service vulnerabilities and external entity exploits (From the library documentation).

Unfortunately, the implementation of this protection mechanism seems to be relatively permissive:
As it can be seen at the first lines of the xmltodict.py the library tries to import the secure library "defusedexpat.pyexpat" but in case when an error occurs (say for example the library isn't installed) the vulnerable "xml.parsers.expat" is loaded, leaving the code vulnerable to an XXE attack.

try:
    from defusedexpat import pyexpat as expat
except ImportError:
    from xml.parsers import expat

issue_title: Expose models via RPC?
issue_body:
Hi folks,
I have a decent size project at https://github.com/kiwitcms/Kiwi which does have a custom grown XML-RPC interface (legacy code) pretty much all of which exposes the available models and variations of the filter/get/create and update methods. The underlying methods boil down to performing a query, iterating over it and serializing every object as a dictionary. Then there's a Python API client which duplicates most of this structure in order to read the serialized data and turn it back into something more Pythonic.

How hard would be to expose models over RPC with modernrpc ? If that is doable I'd rather spend my time contributing a PR to you and dropping all the legacy stuff in Kiwi instead of manually going through each of our exposed RPC methods and figuring out of there are duplicates or not.

issue_title: Output Element Height
issue_body:
Known issue with Output Elements.

If the `Output `Element is on the same row as another element that is **higher** in height than the `Output` Element, then the Output Element and the its scrollbar height will be the same as the highest element in that row.  

Perhaps the fix is in how the Output element is packed into the row.

Could be a delay in solving this as the solution is very similar to a potential design for columns.

issue_title: Taking redis client from django.core.cache
issue_body:
Hello there. I have a little suggestion for the package. It is not so cool to re-declare your Redis url when you already have it on the settings. I have a quick workaround and would like your opinion.

First, add a new setting to the package: `DEFENDER_REDIS_NAME`. Is the name of the cache you will use, taken from the `CACHES` django setting.

Second, some code:

- defender/connection.py
```python
from django.core.cache import caches

def get_redis_connection():
    ...
    elif config.DEFENDER_REDIS_NAME:
        return caches[config.DEFENDER_REDIS_NAME].get_master_client()
    ...
```
Tried it and worked fine. If you are not against it, I can create a PR really quick.

issue_title: Need to add keyword parameter 'timeout'
issue_body:
The utilized pyodbc library uses keyword parameter 'timeout' in call to 'connect' to set the timeout of connection attempt. Zero value means default timeout, just like without keyword parameter.
It is desirable to allow the user to set the timeout optionally, perhaps by adding it in 'OPTIONS'.
Example:

```python
    DATABASES = {
        'default': {
            'ENGINE': 'sql_server.pyodbc',
            'NAME': 'guard',
            'USER': 'test',
            'PASSWORD': 'test',
            'OPTIONS': {
                'driver': 'SQL Server Native Client 10.0',
                'dsn': 'GuardLocal',
                'unicode_results': True,
                'timeout': 1,  # notice this line
            },
        },
    }
```

This requires one small change in Database.connect call in base.py. It should be changed from
```python
conn = Database.connect(connstr, unicode_results=unicode_results)
```
to
```python
conn = Database.connect(connstr, unicode_results=unicode_results, timeout=options.get('timeout', 0))
```

issue_title: IOError(interrupted system call) on dropping privilages
issue_body:
This is the same issue referenced here, I believe.
https://github.com/mopidy/mopidy/issues/718

The errors are IOErrors coming from the line
                events = self.poller.poll(timeout=self.timeout)
of the EPollPoller in manager.py

The errors appear on resume from suspend and when my process drops root(I am using Linux mint on a macbook)

My current workaround is to wrap that line in a try block, and treat IOErrors as if poll() had returned [ ].
I assume since the code does not specify edge polling, that any missed events will just be caught on the next poll.

I briefly tested the latest master but the issue appeared to still be present, my apologies if It's my fault and it's already been fixed.

I think on Windows it's necessary to open the new (target) file and fsync it.

issue_body:
Hey,
We are using `hvad` with DRF and it's amazing, thanks for the library and your time in making it.

We've run into a small issue when updating to the new release. One of our models has a `GenericRelation` to an `AuditLog` model: `audit_logs = GenericRelation(AuditLog)`, and with the new release it triggers the following error when saving:

```python
  File "/home/tom/.virtualenvs/doge/lib/python3.4/site-packages/rest_framework/mixins.py", line 74, in perform_update
    serializer.save()
  File "/home/tom/.virtualenvs/doge/lib/python3.4/site-packages/rest_framework/serializers.py", line 186, in save
    self.instance = self.update(self.instance, validated_data)
  File "/home/tom/.virtualenvs/doge/lib/python3.4/site-packages/hvad/contrib/restframework/serializers.py", line 239, in update
    return super(TranslatableModelMixin, self).update(instance, data)
  File "/home/tom/.virtualenvs/doge/lib/python3.4/site-packages/hvad/contrib/restframework/serializers.py", line 124, in update
    self.update_translation(instance, translation_data)
  File "/home/tom/.virtualenvs/doge/lib/python3.4/site-packages/hvad/contrib/restframework/serializers.py", line 144, in update_translation
    instance.save(update_fields=fields)
  File "/home/tom/.virtualenvs/doge/lib/python3.4/site-packages/hvad/models.py", line 278, in save
    translation.save(*args, **tkwargs)
  File "/home/tom/.virtualenvs/doge/lib/python3.4/site-packages/django_fsm/__init__.py", line 482, in save
    super(ConcurrentTransitionMixin, self).save(*args, **kwargs)

    % ', '.join(non_model_fields))
ValueError: The following fields do not exist in this model or are m2m fields: audit_logs
```

As far as I can tell the problem lies within the `update_translation` method in `restframework/serializers.py`:

```python

issue_title: inconsistency with librosa
issue_body:
I compared the mfcc of [librosa](https://github.com/librosa/librosa/) with [python_speech_analysis](https://github.com/jameslyons/python_speech_features) package and got **totally different results**. 

Which one is correct?
librosa list of first frame coefficients:

> [-395.07433842032867, -7.1149347948192963e-14, 3.5772469223901538e-14, -1.7476140989485184e-14, 3.1665300829452658e-14, -4.4214136625668904e-14, 6.7157035631648599e-14, 1.5013974158050108e-14, 2.9512326634271699e-14, 7.2275398398734558e-14, -1.5043753316598812e-13, -2.2358383003147776e-14, 1.6209256159527285e-13]


python_speech_analysis list of first frame coefficients:

> [-169.91598446684722, 1.3219891974654943, 0.22216979881740945, -0.7368248288464827, 0.26268194306407788, 1.8470757480486224, 3.2670900572694435, 2.3726120692753563, 1.4983949546889608, 0.67862219561000914, -0.44705590991616034, 0.39184067109778226, -0.48048214059101707]


```
import librosa
import python_speech_features
from scipy.signal.windows import hann

n_mfcc = 13
n_mels = 40
n_fft = 512 # in librosa, win_length is assumed to be equal to n_fft implicitly
hop_length = 160
fmin = 0
fmax = None
y, sr = librosa.load(librosa.util.example_audio_file())
sr = 16000  # fake sample rate just to make the point

# librosa
mfcc_librosa = librosa.feature.mfcc(y=y, sr=sr, n_fft=n_fft,
                                    n_mfcc=n_mfcc, n_mels=n_mels,
                                    hop_length=hop_length,
                                    fmin=fmin, fmax=fmax)

# python_speech_features
# no preemph nor ceplifter in librosa, so setting to zero
# librosa default stft window is hann
mfcc_speech = python_speech_features.mfcc(signal=y, samplerate=sr, winlen=n_fft / sr, winstep=hop_length / sr,
                                          numcep=n_mfcc, nfilt=n_mels, nfft=n_fft, lowfreq=fmin, highfreq=fmax,
                                          preemph=0, ceplifter=0, appendEnergy=False, winfunc=hann)


print(list(mfcc_librosa[:, 0]))
print(list(mfcc_speech[0, :]))
```

```sql
SELECT  f_0, f_1 FROM (SELECT  ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY tag.id, tag.title) AS w_row, tag.id AS f_0,  tag.title AS f_1 FROM tag WHERE (tag.id > 0) ) TMP WHERE w_row BETWEEN 2 AND 40;

issue_title: Specify a default timeout that isn't forever
issue_body:
The [Requests library does not have a timeout set by default](http://docs.python-requests.org/en/master/user/advanced/#timeouts). To prevent python-cloudant waiting forever for a HTTP connection or a response we should set a reasonable default timeout on all requests and allow customization.

- Cloudant (python-cloudant) version(s) that are affected by this issue.
    - All 2.x
- Python version
    - Any

issue_title: Use text/plain for foreign key autocomplete admin
issue_body: My admin site uses `ForeignKeyAutocompleteAdmin`. One day, our autocomplete fields started looking like this: The full HTML response looked like this: `My Venue` This issue was that we are using `htmlmin.middleware.HtmlMinifyMiddleware`, and since the response's content type is `text/html`, the response was 'minified' to include HTML tags I solved this issue by doing this in my admin class: ``` def foreignkey_autocomplete(self, request): response = super(BaseModelAdmin, self).foreignkey_autocomplete(request) # HtmlMinifyMiddleware does not play nicely with ForeignKeyAutocompleteAdmin - force response to plain text response['Content-Type'] = 'text/plain' return response ``` Though this code solved my issue, I suggest that the content type of `foreignkey_autocomplete` response should be set to `text/plain` instead of using the default `text/html` to avoid more issues like this.

issue_title: Ability to skip invalidating directories for `touched` children
issue_body:
With watchman `4.5.0` on OSX and a query like:
```
expression=[
  'allof',  # All of the below rules must be true to match.
  ['not', ['pcre', r'^\..*', 'wholename']]  # Exclude files in hidden dirs.
]
```
... `touch`ing a file `b/1` contained in a directory `b/` will invalidate the directory in addition to the file. Presumably this is because a directory listing that includes access/modification times might have changed?

Thanks for building a great system!

issue_title: "jQuery.expr[":"] is now jQuery.expr.pseudos" warning: unclear whether breaking change or just deprecated
issue_body:
According to https://github.com/jquery/jquery-migrate/issues/203#issuecomment-230646733

> In Migrate there are messages saying that some features are deprecated (may be removed in the future) and some saying that they are already removed.

This warning:
```
jQuery.expr[":"] is now jQuery.expr.pseudos
```
doesn't explicitly used either the word "deprecated" or "removed". From the abovementioned comment I kind of get that if something "is now" something else it's probably a breaking change (though jQueryUI triggers this warning and doesn't break). 

Should be more explicit.

If there *are* two classes of warnings, you should use two very precise phrases with keywords that are consistently present in each of them so that they can be searched reliably, besides being 100% unambiguous.

issue_title: Preconditions when setting contents for key using boto3
issue_body:
On boto2, we can store an object using the name of Key object as the key and the preconditions (i.e. If-Match, If-None-Match, etc.) inside the headers dict parameter that will be sent with the PUT request.

Example:

```python
# assume both bucket and key are created beforehand
fp_a = FakeWriteFile(file_size, 'A')
key.set_content_from_file(fp_a, headers={'If-Match': '*'})
```
Now for boto3, there are at least a couple of APIs that would let us put an object inside a bucket such as:

```python
fp_a = FakeWriteFile(file_size, 'A')
bucket.Object('foo').put(Body=fp_a)
```

Alternatively, using low-level S3 client:

```python
bucket.put_object(Key='foo', Body=fp_a) 
```

For the two cases above on boto3, where would I need to add preconditions to ensure that those PUT requests are made through if they meet all the conditions, similarly to boto2's headers parameter with the preconditions set in? I tried to find information on boto3 API DOC, but those preconditions are only set for GET operations: https://boto3.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/services/s3.html

Thanks!

                        'SSECustomerAlgorithm' => 'AES256',
                        'SSECustomerKey'       => $customerKey,
                        'SSECustomerKeyMD5'    => md5($customerKey ,true),
                    ]);
```
The only difference I see here is that php's md5 method can take second argument which, if true, returns and 16 character long digest as compared to normal 32 character long digest. Now I don't know how to create a 16 character long digest using hashlib.md5.

issue_title: WebhookEvent.verify method returns "No such digest method" when directly passing in HTTP_PAYPAL_AUTH_ALGO
issue_body:
Looking into it it appears that the header HTTP_PAYPAL_AUTH_ALGO is "SHA256withRSA" but OpenSSL.crypto doesn't recognize this as valid.  I noticed that there is a default value for that argument on WebhookEvent.verify which is "sha256" which IS a recognized digest.  However, the verify method should work if passed the actual http header as specified in the paypal docs.

Digging around a bit it appears that "sha256WithRSAEncryption" is equivalent to "SHA256withRSA" and "sha256WithRSAEncryption" IS a recognized value for OpenSSL.crypto.  I know next to nothing about any of this so I suppose that perhaps these three values are all synonyms for each other.  

The WebhookEvent.verify method should correctly convert the "SHA256withRSA" paypal header message into something that python OpenSSL.crypto understands, however as I'm no expert on this I will not submit a pull request and will let more informed minds figure the best way to resolve this.

issue_title: Why is pyhive not asynchronous?
issue_body:
Filed [this question](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/42331681/why-is-pyhive-not-asynchronous) at stack overflow:

Then there is the results of running that code, which shows it is completely synchronous, despite async=True. Any idea why, or how to make it truly async?

I found [this question](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/35404029/asynchronous-hive-query-execution-operationhandle-gets-cleaned-up-at-server-si#36131073) which might be related, which says it might be TCP, but I'm not sure.

Code:

```#!/usr/bin/env python2

HIVE_HOST = '...'

from pyhive import hive
from TCLIService.ttypes import TOperationState
cursor = hive.connect(HIVE_HOST).cursor()
cursor.execute('SELECT * FROM test', async=True)

status = cursor.poll().operationState
print TOperationState._VALUES_TO_NAMES[status]
loops = 0
while status in (TOperationState.INITIALIZED_STATE, TOperationState.RUNNING_STATE):
    loops += 1
    logs = cursor.fetch_logs()
    for message in logs:
        print message
    status = cursor.poll().operationState
    print status
print "loops %d" % loops
print cursor.fetchall()
```

A run:

```$ ./hive_example.py
FINISHED_STATE
loops 0
.. some data ..
```

issue_title: Allow for specifying xpath locator strategy when using matching xpath keywords
issue_body:
We shouldn't penalize users for having the leading xpath locator strategy "xpath=" in the locator that is passed to the keywords `Get Matching Xpath Count` and `Xpath Should Match X Times`. At my company, for example, it is general practice to create variables for the locators specifying the strategy. Thus when using the matching xpath count keywords we have to make a separate variable. Instead we can simply accept either and give a meaningful error otherwise; something like

```python
if locator.startswith('//')
    locator = 'xpath=' + locator

if locator.startswith('xpath=')
    # use locator
else
    # raise an menaingful error ... "locator should be xpath and start with xpath= or //"...
```

issue_title: Consumer hanging after a broker gets down
issue_body:
Trying the library, I set the following situation:

Five brokers in the kafka cluster, one producer and two consumers over one topic. 

If I stop one of the brokers, the producer get some warning messages and after some seconds, restarts its normal behavior with no problem. The consumer, on the other side, fails with a Socket Disconnect exception on a different thread, leaving the process alive, but not doing anything.

Using what I saw on the producer code, I modified the consumer to use a similar approach:

https://github.com/appsoma/pykafka/pull/2

However, I'm not sure if there were a reason to the behavior of the consumer, if my code breaks something else or if it doesn't fit the code philosophy that you're following.

Excuse my poor English, please.

issue_title: Performance issues
issue_body:
**PyKafka version**: 2.2.0
**Kafka version**: 0.8.2

We have notice performance issues with upgrading from pykafka 2.1.2 to pykafka 2.2.0.

The graph below shows the 1, 5 and 15 minute load averages on one of our production machines with a pykafka 2.2.0 producer running, no producers or consumers, and a pykafka 2.1.2 producer running.

![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/449835/13250546/3d21c334-da33-11e5-8d41-5ce3ea084090.png)

We are still not sure why the load is so high. It might not be cpu bound, but rather socket contention, or something else entirely.

The service on that machine runs with 40 worker processes and between those 40 workers, pykafka 2.2.0 seems to create about 1690 threads, of which 1428 are `queue_reader` threads created in the `OwnedBroker` constructor, and 262 are `worker` threads created in `RequestHandler._start_thread`. It is producing to 1 topic with 6 partitions on a 3-broker Kafka cluster.

The `queue_reader` threads are created here:

```
  File "/workspace/venv/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pykafka/topic.py", line 88, in get_producer
    return Cls(self._cluster, self, **kwargs)
  File "/workspace/venv/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pykafka/producer.py", line 152, in __init__
    self.start()
  File "/workspace/venv/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pykafka/producer.py", line 189, in start
    self._setup_owned_brokers()
  File "/workspace/venv/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pykafka/producer.py", line 228, in _setup_owned_brokers
    self, partition.leader)
  File "/workspace/venv/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pykafka/producer.py", line 446, in __init__
    self.producer._cluster.handler.spawn(queue_reader)
  File "/workspace/venv/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pykafka/handlers.py", line 85, in spawn
    t.start()
```

and the `worker` threads are created here:

```
  File "/workspace/venv/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pykafka/cluster.py", line 66, in __getitem__
    meta = self._cluster()._get_metadata([key])
  File "/workspace/venv/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pykafka/cluster.py", line 254, in _get_metadata
    metadata = self._request_metadata(broker_connects, topics)
  File "/workspace/venv/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pykafka/cluster.py", line 232, in _request_metadata
    source_port=self._source_port)
  File "/workspace/venv/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pykafka/broker.py", line 92, in __init__
    self.connect()
  File "/workspace/venv/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pykafka/broker.py", line 202, in connect
    self._req_handler.start()
  File "/workspace/venv/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pykafka/handlers.py", line 128, in start
    self.t = self._start_thread()
  File "/workspace/venv/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pykafka/handlers.py", line 165, in _start_thread
    return self.handler.spawn(worker)
  File "/workspace/venv/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pykafka/handlers.py", line 85, in spawn
    t.start()
```

Any help with why there might be so many threads, how that might impact performance and if there is something we can do about it would be very helpful.

issue_title: denoise_bilateral works separately on channels, but no warning raised for 3-D images
issue_body:
The implementation of ``restoration.denoise_bilateral`` is 2-D only, with the last dimension considered to be for channels. However, this is not documented in the docstring, and no warning is raised if a 3-D image (with last dimension > 3) is passed. 

I used ``denoise_bilateral`` in the past on 3-D images thinking that the implementation was 3-D. We could consider making the code 3-D or nD in the future, but for now fixing the docstring + warning looks like a reasonable goal for 0.12.

issue_title: GeospatialCoordinateEncoder "altitude" seems optional, but not really
issue_body:
I ran into difficulties generating anomaly likelihood values with a GeospatialCoordinateEncoder (GCE), and I believe the root cause is that the `altitude` portion of the raw input vector.

In all current examples of GCE usage, the input vector is [created like this](https://github.com/numenta/nupic.geospatial/blob/master/model/geospatial_anomaly.py#L136-L138):

```python
modelInput = {
    "vector": (speed, longitude, latitude, altitude)
}
```

... where `altitude` may be `None`. This starts breaking when you use the anomaly likelihood post-process, because it introduces arrays with `None` values, and numpy complains loudly when trying to mean the data:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/Users/mtaylor/Library/Python/2.7/lib/python/site-packages/nupic/algorithms/anomaly_likelihood.py", line 208, in anomalyProbability
    skipRecords=numSkipRecords)
  File "/Users/mtaylor/Library/Python/2.7/lib/python/site-packages/nupic/algorithms/anomaly_likelihood.py", line 364, in estimateAnomalyLikelihoods
    performLowerBoundCheck=False)
  File "/Users/mtaylor/Library/Python/2.7/lib/python/site-packages/nupic/algorithms/anomaly_likelihood.py", line 578, in estimateNormal
    "mean": numpy.mean(sampleData),
  File "/System/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/Extras/lib/python/numpy/core/fromnumeric.py", line 2716, in mean
    out=out, keepdims=keepdims)
  File "/System/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/Extras/lib/python/numpy/core/_methods.py", line 62, in _mean
    ret = um.add.reduce(arr, axis=axis, dtype=dtype, out=out, keepdims=keepdims)
TypeError: unsupported operand type(s) for +: 'float' and 'NoneType'
```

One workaround is to use `0` for altitude, but this drastically decreases performance by adding another dimension to the input that doesn't really exist.

If you look at how the GCE [encodes input data](https://github.com/numenta/nupic/blob/master/src/nupic/encoders/geospatial_coordinate.py#L81-L90):

```python
altitude = None
if len(inputData) == 4:
  (speed, longitude, latitude, altitude) = inputData
else:
  (speed, longitude, latitude) = inputData
coordinate = self.coordinateForPosition(longitude, latitude, altitude)
radius = self.radiusForSpeed(speed)
super(GeospatialCoordinateEncoder, self).encodeIntoArray(
 (coordinate, radius), output)
```

You can see that `altitude` is **optionally** included in the coordinate. However, [the description](https://github.com/numenta/nupic/blob/master/src/nupic/encoders/geospatial_coordinate.py#L69-L71) **always** returns the altitude:

```python
  def getDescription(self):
    """See `nupic.encoders.base.Encoder` for more information."""
    return [('speed', 0), ('longitude', 1), ('latitude', 2), ('altitude', 3)]
```

I believe this is the root problem, but I'm not sure how to fix it. To get things running for my current project, I have just manually removed the `('altitude', 3)` from the description array, and I can now run NuPIC without an `altitude` and post-process the anomaly likelihood properly.

issue_title: access: CFG_SUPERADMINROLE_ID is not accurate in the demosite.
issue_body:
The access module uses the `CFG_SUPERADMINROLE_ID` config variable to determine what role ID to add to authorisation requests: https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio/blob/maint-2.1/invenio/modules/access/control.py#L1474, but in the standard Invenio demosite installation, the `superadmin` role ID is 11.  This causes unexpected behaviour when authorizing users belonging to whichever role has ID 1.

## Environment
* Invenio `maint-2.1`
* Python `2.7.6`
* System: Ubuntu Trusty (`ubuntu/trusty64` Vagrant image)

## Steps to reproduce
With a standard Invenio demosite installation:
```
mysql> select id, name, description from accROLE where name = 'superadmin';
+----+------------+---------------------------+
| id | name       | description               |
+----+------------+---------------------------+
| 11 | superadmin | superuser with all rights |
+----+------------+---------------------------+
```
```
$ inveniomanage shell
>>> from invenio.modules.access.control import acc_find_possible_roles
>>> acc_find_possible_roles('viewstatistics', always_add_superadmin=True) # Should include superadmin role with ID 11
intbitset([1, 22])
```

### Expected behaviour
Returned `intbitset` object includes 11 (role id of `superadmin`).

### Actual behaviour
Returned `intbitset` object does not include 11, and includes 1 instead.

## Consequent bugs
Users belonging to the actual role with ID 1, in this case `thesescurator`, may perform any action.  An example user is `romeo`, with ID 5:
```
$ inveniomanage shell
>>> from invenio.modules.access.engine import acc_authorize_action
>>> acc_authorize_action(5, 'usedeposit')
(0, 'Authorization granted')
>>> acc_authorize_action(5, 'moderatecomments')
(0, 'Authorization granted')
>>> acc_authorize_action(5, 'non_existant_action')
(0, 'Authorization granted')
```

## Suggestion
Replace with a call to `acc_get_role_id(SUPERADMINROLE)`.  Was there a reason this approach was not taken in the first place?

issue_title: Factor analysis differs when grouping assets by sector
issue_body:
Analyzing a factor with assets grouped by sector yields different results than analyzing without grouping. 

The following code reproduces the issue:
```
import alphalens
from quantopian.pipeline import Pipeline
from quantopian.research import run_pipeline
from quantopian.pipeline.filters.morningstar import Q1500US
from quantopian.pipeline.classifiers.morningstar import Sector
from quantopian.pipeline.data.morningstar import operation_ratios

MORNINGSTAR_SECTOR_CODES = {
     -1: 'Misc',
    101: 'Basic Materials',
    102: 'Consumer Cyclical',
    103: 'Financial Services',
    104: 'Real Estate',
    205: 'Consumer Defensive',
    206: 'Healthcare',
    207: 'Utilities',
    308: 'Communication Services',
    309: 'Energy',
    310: 'Industrials',
    311: 'Technology' ,    
}

def make_pipeline():
    factor_1 = operation_ratios.operation_margin.latest
    
    universe = (Q1500US() 
                & factor_1.notnull())
    
    pipe = Pipeline(
        columns={
            'factor': factor_1.rank(mask=universe, method='average'),
            'Sector': Sector(mask=universe),
        },
        screen=universe
    )
    return pipe

result = run_pipeline(make_pipeline(), start_date='2013-12-01', end_date='2014-12-01')
result.head()

assets = result.index.levels[1].unique()
pricing = get_pricing(assets, start_date='2013-12-01', end_date='2015-01-01', fields='open_price')

alphalens.tears.create_factor_tear_sheet(factor=result['factor'],
                                         prices=pricing,
                                         quantiles=2,
                                         periods=(1,5,10,30))

factor_data = alphalens.utils.get_clean_factor_and_forward_returns(result['factor'],
                                                                   pricing,
                                                                   groupby=result["Sector"],
                                                                   quantiles=2,
                                                                   periods=(1,5,10,30),
                                                                   groupby_labels=MORNINGSTAR_SECTOR_CODES
                                                                   )

alphalens.tears.create_full_tear_sheet(factor_data, 
                                       group_adjust=True, 
                                       by_group=True)
```

issue_title: Add strict validation on body
Don't send back entire schema after validation errors
issue_body:
### Description

When Connexion validates incoming data and finds a property is missing, it will dutifully report this back to the caller. However, it will include the entire swagger spec/schema as part of the response. For large APIs, this can be tens of KB of text, which is not ideal for debugging or logging.

### Expected behaviour

Expect to only receive the error portion, eg: "Missing required property 'email'"

### Actual behaviour

I receive the error portion following by a long JSON string that is my Swagger spec.

### Steps to reproduce

The actual generation of this error appears to occur in *jsonschema*, but this can be solved within Connexion by only sending back the `.message` attribute in the `problem()` call, rather than the string-representation of the entire exception object.

    def validate_schema(self, data):
        """
        :type schema: dict
        :rtype: flask.Response | None
        """
        if self.is_null_value_valid and is_null(data):
            return None

        try:
            validate(data, self.schema, format_checker=draft4_format_checker)
        except ValidationError as exception:
            logger.error("%s validation error: %s" % (flask.request.url, exception))
            return problem(400, 'Bad Request', str(exception))

        return None

Instead, the second-to-last line could read:

            return problem(400, 'Bad Request', exception.message)

Which will return the expected (and succinct) error message without the Swagger spec included.


### Additional info:

Output of the commands:

- `python --version`
  - Python 3.5.1

- `pip show connexion | grep "^Version\:"`
  - Version: 1.0.88

issue_title: OAuth requests fails when server requires a client certificate
issue_body:
Our server requires mutual client/server authentication.  When I was running the current jira-python 2.0.0 version I was getting the following error:

```
WARNING:root:HTTPSConnectionPool(host=HOSTNAME, port=443): Max retries exceeded with url: /rest/api/2/serverInfo (Caused by SSLError(SSLError(1, '[SSL: SSLV3_ALERT_HANDSHAKE_FAILURE] sslv3 alert handshake failure (_ssl.c:833)'),)) while doing GET https://HOSTNAME/rest/api/2/serverInfo [{'params': None, 'headers': {'User-Agent': 'python-requests/2.19.1', 'Accept-Encoding': 'gzip, deflate', 'Accept': 'application/json,*.*;q=0.9', 'Connection': 'keep-alive', 'Cache-Control': 'no-cache', 'Content-Type': 'application/json', 'X-Atlassian-Token': 'no-check'}}]
WARNING:root:Got ConnectionError [HTTPSConnectionPool(host=HOSTNAME, port=443): Max retries exceeded with url: /rest/api/2/serverInfo (Caused by SSLError(SSLError(1, '[SSL: SSLV3_ALERT_HANDSHAKE_FAILURE] sslv3 alert handshake failure (_ssl.c:833)'),))] errno:None on GET https://HOSTNAME/rest/api/2/serverInfo
{'response': None, 'request': <PreparedRequest [GET]>}\{'response': None, 'request': <PreparedRequest [GET]>}
```

I was able to get the code working outside of the Jira client and after a little searching I realized that the client certificate was not being sent to the OAuth1 object being created.

In the def _create_oauth_session(self, oauth, timeout) method of the client.py file, the verify option is pulled out from self._options but the cert option is ignored.  The cert option also should be pulled out and added for client authentication so the code should look as follows:

```
def _create_oauth_session(self, oauth, timeout):
        verify = self._options['verify']
        cert = self._options['client_cert']# ADDED LINE

        from oauthlib.oauth1 import SIGNATURE_RSA
        from requests_oauthlib import OAuth1
        cert = oauth['cert']
        oauth = OAuth1(
            oauth['consumer_key'],
            rsa_key=oauth['key_cert'],
            signature_method=SIGNATURE_RSA,
            resource_owner_key=oauth['access_token'],
            resource_owner_secret=oauth['access_token_secret'])
        self._session = ResilientSession(timeout)
        self._session.verify = verify
        self._session.cert = cert # ADDED LINE
        self._session.auth = oauth
        # self._session.cert = cert
```

I noticed this was already done in methods such as the _create_http_basic_session method above so it appears to have just been missed here.

**Version Information**
Python Interpreter: 3.6.5
jira-python: 2.0.0
OS: Windows 7

issue_title: Add support for logs of all lambda functions
issue_body:
The `chalice logs` command was written when there was only a single lambda function used as your API handler.  I've found it really useful, but I'd like to be able to use this command for all the lambda functions in my app.

I'd like a `-f/--function-name` arg added that lets me provide the logical resource name of the function whose logs I'd like.


### Example

Given this app:

```python

@app.lambda_function()
def foo(event, context): ...

@app.route('/')
def index(): ...
```

The `chalice logs` command will continue to give me lambda logs for my API handler, but I can now also say:

```
$ chalice logs -f foo
```

and it will give me logs for the `foo` function.  Also I'd like to get https://github.com/aws/chalice/issues/4 added as well so I might need to work out if we want `-f` to mean what `tail -f` means or if we just want to use `--tail`.

2018-02-07 13:05:12,907: [111510] m.ethereum:INFO: Finished symbolic transaction: 1 | Code Coverage: 100% | Terminated States: 5 | Alive States: 1
```

With this output, it is difficult to distinguish between states that are expected and states that are of concern. Some of these REVERTs are totally expected, for example the reverts due to an invalid function ID, or passing ether to a non payable function. For these special cases where the REVERT is totally ok and expected, it would be improved UX to express that in the output, for example:

```
2018-02-07 13:05:07,300: [111510] m.main:INFO: Beginning analysis
2018-02-07 13:05:07,361: [111510] m.ethereum:INFO: Starting symbolic transaction: 1
2018-02-07 13:05:07,640: [111510] m.ethereum:INFO: Generated testcase No. 0 - REVERT (invalid function id)
2018-02-07 13:05:09,028: [111510] m.ethereum:INFO: Generated testcase No. 1 - REVERT (non payable function)
2018-02-07 13:05:10,280: [111510] m.ethereum:INFO: Generated testcase No. 2 - REVERT
2018-02-07 13:05:11,566: [111510] m.ethereum:INFO: Generated testcase No. 3 - THROW
2018-02-07 13:05:12,907: [111510] m.ethereum:INFO: Finished symbolic transaction: 1 | Code Coverage: 100% | Terminated States: 5 | Alive States: 1
```

In this output, the user would be table to tell that testcases 2 and 3 are unexpected and should be investigated further.

In order to implement this, we can analyze the constraints when we encounter a revert. For example if we reach a revert and there's a single NOT-EQ constraint on the first 4 bytes, we might be able to detect that this was the expected revert for the invalid function id. if there is a single constraint on the function id, and a single constraint on the callvalue, we might be able to tell that this is the expected non-payable function revert.

issue_title: state.generate_testcase does not use the filename provided
issue_body:
### Manticore version
Version: 0.1.7 (48f88be7590c7b15ff27cfd8c2da883d71e98190)

### Summary of the problem
`state.generate_testcase` does not use the filename provided to generate the files.
This was tested only for smart contracts.

### Step to reproduce the behavior
```
# simple.py
from manticore.ethereum import ManticoreEVM

m = ManticoreEVM() # initiate the blockchain
source_code ='''
pragma solidity^0.4.18;
contract Simple {
    function f(uint a) payable {
        if (a == 0x41) {
            throw;
        }
    }
}
'''

# Initiate the accounts
user_account = m.create_account(balance=1000)
contract_account = m.solidity_create_contract(source_code, owner=user_account, balance=0)

# Call f(a), with a symbolic value
contract_account.f(m.SValue)

# Check if an execution ends with a REVERT or INVALID
for state in m.terminated_states: 
    last_tx = state.platform.transactions[-1]
    if last_tx.result in ['REVERT','INVALID']:
        print "Error found in f() execution (see %s)"%m.workspace

        # filename should be named SimpleBug_00000000.tx
        state.generate_testcase('SimpleBug')
        break
```
`python simple.py` should generate files named "SimpleBug_*", but "test_*" is used insted

### Expected behavior
Generation of mcore_*/SimpleBug* files

### Actual behavior
Generation of mcore_*/test* files

### Any relevant logs
If I use ``m._generate_testcase_callback(state, 'SimpleBug','message')`` and this fix: https://github.com/montyly/manticore/commit/4f3a7ee059f6225bbe51275594e3c14a836a0795
 the files are correclty generated.

issue_title: ManticoreEVM.transaction should safely return if there are no running states
issue_body:
This is in reference to this comment/diff: https://github.com/trailofbits/manticore/pull/715#discussion_r164883644

That diff which added the `if not self.running_state_ids` to `multi_tx_analysis` was removed from Pr #715 since that was somewhat irrelevant to the dynargs feature. A separate pr should be created to handle this situation in the Manticore core; not the the `multi_tx_analysis` function, which is a very high level function, that is essentially user code. Handling it in the core would mean users of the api that are manually calling `.transaction` would also benefit.

currently we raise an assertion error if there are no running states and no initial state
https://github.com/trailofbits/manticore/blob/master/manticore/ethereum.py#L940

issue_title: issue with _interpolate_azimuth_edges
issue_body:
Hi,

I encountered a very weird issue with the function _half_angle_complex in transforms.py.

If the azimuth angles of the rays are all the same, due to rounding the dot_product may be larger than one and therefore produce a nan value. In the newest versions of matplotlib pcolormesh cannot handle masked values for the x or y arguments and therefore it raises an exception.

I solved the issue simply imposing that the dot_product cannot be larger than one:

def _half_angle_complex(complex_angle1, complex_angle2):
    """
    Return half the angle between complex numbers on the unit circle.

    Parameters
    ----------
    complex_angle1, complex_angle2 : complex
        Complex numbers representing unit vectors on the unit circle

    Returns
    -------
    half_angle : float
        Half the angle between the unit vectors in degrees.

    """
    dot_product = np.real(complex_angle1 * np.conj(complex_angle2))
    if dot_product > 1:
        warnings.warn('dot_product larger than one')
        dot_product = 1.
    full_angle_rad = np.arccos(dot_product)
    half_angle_rad = full_angle_rad / 2.
    half_angle_deg = np.rad2deg(half_angle_rad)
    return half_angle_deg

You can test the issue by checking what happen with angle 274. with regular float precision.

I hope I am clear.

issue_title: Storage: Blob.exists() does not work within Batch context
issue_body:
The `Blob.exists()` method does not work when run within a Batch context. The normal behavior of `exists()` is to return True unless a `NotFound` exception occurs. Within the Batch context the exception seems to be suppressed and the function returns True. After leaving the Batch context, an Exception is then thrown.

This is how I expected to be able to use the `exists()` function:

```python
blobs = [storage.blob.Blob(path, bucket) for path in paths]
with client.batch():
  bools = [blob.exists() for blob in blobs]
```

Without the `Batch` contextmanager this code works, if inefficiently. With the `Batch` contextmanager the code returns all `True`s and throws an exception when leaving the context.

This behavior seems unintuitive to me. Please let me know if the API is meant to be used differently. If it is meant to be used as in the provided code sample, I'd be happy to attempt a fix if one of the maintainers could point me in the right direction.

Environment configuration just in case:
- macOS 10.13.3
- Python 3.6.5
- google-cloud-storage==1.8.0

issue_title: Trace: Implement V2 API client
issue_body:
As the [Stackdriver Trace V2 API](https://cloud.google.com/trace/docs/reference/v2/rpc/google.devtools.cloudtrace.v2) is launched, we need to implement client library to support using it.

Checklist for this issue:

- [x] Merge the Autogen Trace V2 library (PR #4379)
- [x] Implement Stackdriver Trace V2 API client

After the Trace V2 API client library is available, calling `from google.cloud import trace` will by default using the V2 API. To use V1 API, one will need to explicitly import like this: `from google.cloud.trace.v1 import Client`.

issue_title: Create_results_dirs validity requirement could frsutrate callers
issue_body:
I added a patch to the cache_manager that checks validity of the results_dirs. But that insistence on passing the validity check inside the invalidation block can put independent callers in an unresolvable state.

This is only going to bite a specific type of use case.

I caught it in a plugin that uses a custom fingerprint while also managing its own caching. cache_target_dirs is false and so after entering the invalidation block, this task called vt.create_resultz_dirs, in order to drop a subset of its output there, while still allowing the artifact cache to manage checking for and resolving hits.

But in that case, the VT is valid after the block. So calling into the create_results_dirs fails, since it only assumes the dirs exist and hard fail if that assumption is incorrect.

I think the fix should be to only do validity enforcement at update time, as it was in the first  revision. If a task is taking cache management into their own hands, they should be given the benefit of the entire invalidation block to handle their business. Only after the task exits in an illegal state should the exception surface.

I have a (non-bugfix) follow-up about done, I will write up a separate test/fix for this as well.

issue_title: exclude-target-regexp not applied consistently in v2
issue_body:
The v2 engine does not apply the `--exclude-target-regexp` option consistently... or, perhaps, it applies it _more_ consistently than v1 does.

As discovered in https://github.com/twitter/commons/pull/451 , the v1 engine apparently applies the excludes to target roots, whereas v2 applies them for all requests for a target.

issue_title: Support deprecation for more than just options
issue_body:
The discussion in https://rbcommons.com/s/twitter/r/2650/ put us on a path where it would be nice if we could deprecate things external to pants (pants.ini config, command-line options, things in BUILD files) in such a way that users upgrading from any arbitrary version of pants, to any future version of pants, could be notified in the deprecations in a nice way.

This was further discussed in slack:

https://pantsbuild.slack.com/archives/general/p1440451305004760

The idea seems to be to create a tool (either inside of pants or outside), that pants developers can register deprecated things (which will be stored for all time), and that pants admins can use when upgrading pants in their respective repo.

issue_title: When `kolibri start` fails in daemon mode, no indication to user (just in logs)
issue_body:
### Observed behavior

If starting the server fails (e.g. port is already taken), we still tell the user that it started and what addresses it should be available at.

### Expected behavior

If it fails to start, an error should be shown, and we shouldn't tell the user it's available at a certain set of addresses. We can probably wait in the parent process until the child process either errors or sends a success signal (or perhaps just stay in foreground mode and stream stdout/stderr from the child process until the server successfully starts, then go to background).

issue_title: opinel 1.0x seems to have introduced a 75-second delay.
issue_body:
opinel 1.0x seems to have introduced a (roughly) 75-second delay to any anything making calls to the AWS SDK, whether running one of the `AWS-recipes` Python scripts or running `Scout2`.

For instance, when running `aws_recipes_assume_role.py`, there's a 75-second delay before results are returned and the credentials are cached in the `~/.aws/cli/cache` directory.

When running `aws_recipes_init_sts_session.py`, there's a 75-second delay before it prompts for the MFA code.

When running `aws_recipes_create_ip_ranges.py` it says "Fetching public IP information for the 'xxxxxxx' environment..." and then pauses for 75 seconds before it starts iterating through the various regions and displaying "...in us-east-1: EC2 instances", etc.

After that initial 75-second delay, everything runs as expected. (For instance, there's not an additional 75-second delay for each step of the Scout2 report generation process, only at the very beginning.)

And running the scripts or Scout2 with the `--debug` option returns no additional information. The scripts are succeeding; they just take 75 seconds longer than expected.

issue_title: Provide an interface with further actions when a URI-R is found
issue_body:
The current implementation of ipwb replay return an HTTP 404 without a response body (using Flask) when a URI-R is requested but not found. This might occur through URL hacking, which we want to encourage using for exploration.

It would be useful to, first off, provide a response body to guide the user when encountering a 404 using the replay system. Secondary, provide something action-able for the user to do from within this body, e.g., a link to the main replay home page or an input box to try other URIs.

Another good solution might be to provide some sort of "Archive Now" functionality through the web UI. This might invoke some other script to fetch the contents on the live web at the URI, push to ipfs, write the cdxj, and make available for immediate replay.

A few tasks and discussion are needed for this.

1. Should we even get into the business of capture? (per discussion w/ @ibnesayeed )
2. ipwb replay currently relies on the cdxj being specified at startup, no means exist to supplement or replace the index being used at runtime.
3. A user will already have Python (assumably) if running ipwb, are there tools to get a good representation cf. scraping tools e.g., wget 
4. This content never needs to be pushed into a WARC, which would allow the procedure to use anything that can read an HTTP response body, ideally pre/post-JS processing.
